journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Promoting and impeding factors for exercise in patients with osteopenia or osteoporosis: A systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aSchool of Nursing, Xinjiang Medical University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, China
bThe Sixth Affiliated Hospital of Xinjiang Medical University, Urumqi 830002, Xinjiang, China
cXinjiang Regional Research Center for Population Disease and Health Care, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, China

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Osteopenia and osteoporosis are pressing public health issues associated with aging.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To systematically identify and synthesize the facilitators and barriers to exercise intervention adherence among individuals with osteopenia or osteoporosis.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: Systematic review

sec.title: Data Sources
sec.p: PubMed, Cochrane, EMBASE, Medline (Ovid), and Web of Science from inception to 31 March 2025.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Inclusion criteria: (1) Population: individuals with osteopenia/osteoporosis; (2) Intervention: exercise interventions targeting osteopenia/osteoporosis-related impairments; (3) Comparison: clear control group; (4) Outcomes: primary (adherence facilitators/barriers, e.g., pedometer/log data); secondary (dropout/adherence, adherence-promotion strategies, participant views); (5) Study type: randomized controlled trials. Exclusion criteria: (1) studies that were duplicates or had overlapping datasets; (2) incomplete publications/insufficient extractable data; (3) non-English literature. Two reviewers independently extracted data in duplicate and assessed bias risk.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Thirty randomized controlled trials tested median 31-week programs (aerobic, balance, resistance, flexibility, impact, or functional training). Participants adhered to 83 % (59–100 %) of sessions; "lack of time" was the primary barrier (n=11/30) . Nine trials had low overall bias risk; allocation concealment or participant blinding was infrequently reported.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: For individuals with osteopenia or osteoporosis, key barriers to activity were lack of time and existing health issues. Conversely, facilitators included expert coaching, engaging routines, group camaraderie, accessible facilities, and safety precautions. Future researchers should explore bone health-optimized exercises, identify activity facilitators/barriers, and develop tailored approaches to sustain patients’ fitness regimens.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: The review protocol was registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO registration CRD420251013691).

sec.title: Tweetable Abstract
sec.p: Exercise boosts bone health but poor adherence limits efficacy. Our systematic review identifies top facilitators and barriers to optimize adherence.

list-item.p: Osteoporosis is a major public health problem affecting the elderly, especially postmenopausal women.
Exercise intervention is of great significance to improve bone health and reduce fracture risk in these patients.
Researchers have shown that exercise increases bone mineral density, muscle strength, and balance while lowering fall risk, but poor adherence impairs its effectiveness.

We have identified key facilitators (professional guidance, social support, engaging programs, accessible amenities) and barriers (time constraints, personal health issues) to exercise adherence in individuals with osteopenia/osteoporosis.
Classifying these factors via the Behavior Change Wheel provides a theoretical basis for developing targeted strategies to improve long-term exercise adherence in this population.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Osteoporosis is a systemic bone disease characterized by bone fragility resulting from reduced bone mass (Compston et al., 2019). It predominantly affects postmenopausal women and older men. Osteopenia refers to a specific range of bone mineral density. Its T-score falls between –1.0 and –2.5 standard deviations below the mean for young adults. This measurement is obtained via dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Porter et al., 2023). Epidemiological data suggest that in 2020, 18.3 % of the global population suffered from osteoporosis, with women being nearly twice as likely to develop the disease (23.1 %) as men (11.7 %) (Salari et al., 2021). This substantial sex difference underscores the particular susceptibility of women to bone density loss as they age. Often, osteoporosis remains undiagnosed in its early stages because of the lack of obvious symptoms, leading many patients to discover they have the condition only after experiencing fractures. Older adults, in particular, tend to underestimate the severity and consequences of osteoporosis, resulting in missed opportunities for timely prevention and treatment (Bougioukli et al., 2019). This neglect not only heightens the risk of fractures—the most severe complication of the disease—but also takes a significant toll on patients' mental well-being and overall quality of life (Klotzbuecher et al., 2010; Nuti et al., 2019). Beyond individual suffering, osteoporosis places a substantial financial strain on families and society as a whole (Svedbom et al., 2013).

At present, the treatment of osteoporosis is mainly drug administration. The long-term use of bisphosphonates, a first-line drug for the treatment of osteoporosis, may lead to atypical femoral fractures, bisphosphonate-related mandibular necrosis, and other adverse reactions. These adverse reactions will not disappear even after drug withdrawal (Lu et al., 2020). Osteoporosis is preventable and treatable, so it is still necessary to find new ways to improve the prognosis of patients. Exercise therapy as a means of prevention and treatment can increase bone mineral density in patients with osteoporosis and improve the quality of life of patients (Layne and Nelson, 1999).

In exploring the facilitators and barriers to exercise adherence, the Behaviour Change Wheel theory offers a systematic analytical framework(Chen et al., 2025). This framework helps comprehensively understand the multifaceted factors that influence patients' exercise adherence. The Behavior Change Wheel, developed by Michie et al. (2011), synthesizes 19 behavior-change frameworks. Its core comprises three layers: the Capability-Opportunity-Motivation behavior system posits that behavior emerges from the interaction of capability, opportunity, and motivation. The capability covers psychological and physiological levels, the opportunity involves physical and social factors, and the motivation includes reflective and spontaneous types. The middle nine intervention functions, such as education and motivation, are targeted to solve the lack of capability, opportunity, and motivation; the outer seven policy categories, such as legislation and fiscal measures, provide support for intervention (Michie et al., 2011). We used the nine behavior change techniques of the Behavior Change Wheel to categorize the facilitators and barriers to physical activity.

Additionally, exercise can enhance muscle strength, improve body balance, and reduce the risk of falls, which is essential for the prevention of osteoporotic fractures. Although the importance of exercise intervention in the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis has been widely recognized, many researchers have shown that exercise intervention has failed to achieve the desired results due to the generally low exercise adherence of patients (Bae et al., 2023). As of March 2025, research studies on barriers and facilitators of exercise adherence in patients with osteoporosis remain limited. Previous reviews were mostly limited to the surface description of influencing factors, while we systematically used the behavioral change wheel framework for the first time to classify and analyze related barriers and facilitator factors to provide a theoretical basis for subsequent intervention design (Benedetti et al., 2018). The primary purpose of this study was to examine the extent to which randomized controlled trials employed exercise interventions in patients with osteoporosis: (1) to examine the facilitators and barriers to exercise adherence, and (2) to report exercise adherence rates.

sec.title: Protocol and registration
sec.p: This systematic review was registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) at http://www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/ on March 18, 2025, under registration number CRD420251013691.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: We searched five bibliographic databases for systematic reviews: PubMed, Cochrane, EMBASE, Medline (Ovid), and Web of Science, from inception to March 2025. The key search terms were as follows: (“exercise*” or “sport*” or “physical activity*” or “train*” or “aerobic*”) and (“osteoporosis*” or “bone loss” or “low bone mass”). A summary of the search strategies is presented in Supplementary Material.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p:
Eligibility criteria were established to include interventions that required adherence to exercise programs and to identify the factors that influenced whether people with osteoporosis participated in and adhered to the program. The exact inclusion criteria are provided in Table 1. Exclusion criteria: (1) research studies that were duplicates or had overlapping datasets; (2) publications with incomplete content or insufficient data for extraction; (3) literature not published in English.Table 1
Inclusion criteria for studies on exercise interventions for osteopenia and osteoporosis.Table 1:
Population●According to the diagnostic criteria established by the World Health Organization, osteopenia is diagnosed based on the T-score measured by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry, with the specific criterion being −2.5 < T-score < −1. When the T-score is less than or equal to −2.5, the condition is diagnosed as osteoporosis.
Intervention●Any exercise interventions designed to benefit impairments related to osteopenia or osteoporosis.
Comparison●The study had a clear control group (either sedentary, another exercise intervention, or a sham intervention).
Outcomes●Primary outcomes: (I) facilitators and barriers; (II) measures of exercise adherence (i.e., pedometer, log book).●Secondary outcomes: (I) dropout rates and adherence rates;(II) how exercise adherence was promoted (i.e., teach back method or supervision of program);(III) if participants’ views and experiences of program were taken into account.
Study type●Randomized controlled trials.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: After searching electronic databases, a researcher (LY: Li Yi) exported all studies to reference management software, and other researchers (ZCX: Zhang Chenxin and ZLP: Zhang Liping) rechecked the extracted studies. The titles and abstracts of the extracted studies were independently screened by two researchers (LY and ZCX) according to the inclusion criteria. Subsequently, they separately reviewed the full text to select the final studies for inclusion. If the screening results differed, consensus was achieved through discussion. Another researcher (ZLP) supervised the screening process. The overall kappa score for the title and abstract screening process was 0.79.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: One reviewer (LY) extracted data, which was independently verified by another reviewer (ZCX). The data extraction of the selected studies was performed using a structured form that included year of publication, duration of the exercise intervention, outcome measurements utilized, intervention cost for the participants, exercise instructor qualifications, age and number of participants, average bone mineral density T-score, location/type of exercise facility, socioeconomic status of the participants, participants' views about the exercise program, number of falls occurring during the intervention, methods to promote exercise adherence, methods to measure adherence, drop-out rates, adherence rates to exercise, barriers and facilitators to the exercise program, methodological quality score, and the study’s country of origin. Authors were contacted if information was missing. Informally gathered missing information was not included in our study to maintain consistency between studies.

To address the facilitators and barriers to exercise, we chose to use a theoretical framework to assist with the identification and categorization of these outcomes. In recent years, the Behavior Change Wheel theory has been widely recognized in the field of health behavior intervention, providing strong guidance for many research and practice endeavors. In this research, the Behavior Change Wheel framework was used to systematically identify the factors that promote or hinder exercise adherence in patients with osteoporosis. First, we screened the contributing factors and barriers of all the reports in the randomized controlled trials and combined the conceptually similar items into a preliminary list. Next, we categorized each barrier into one of three domains: Capability, Opportunity, or Motivation-Behavior. Finally, we mapped each barrier to the intervention function recommended by the Behavior Change Wheel intervention.

sec.title: Risk-of-Bias assessment
sec.p: Meta-analysis was not conducted due to the heterogeneity of the reviewed studies’ intervention types, study outcomes, and settings. The risk of bias in each study was assessed and rated using the Review Manager 5.4 tool for randomized controlled trials. The Review Manager 5.4 tool includes six domains: randomization process, deviations from intended intervention, missing outcome data, measurement of outcome, and selection of the reported result. Each of the five domains was rated as low risk of bias, high risk of bias, or some concerns, and the overall risk of bias was judged based on the assessment of each domain (Yang et al., 2024). All studies were rated by one researcher (LY) and checked by another researcher (ZCX), and discrepancies were discussed with a third researcher to reach a consensus.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
The search yielded 8821 records from five databases. After eliminating duplicates and identifying potential studies from reference lists, a total of 5941 unique records were screened by examining titles and abstracts, and 5837 were subsequently excluded. Upon full-text review of 104 articles, 74 were excluded for various reasons. Thirty studies met the inclusion criteria and were included in the final qualitative synthesis. (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
PRISMA Flowchart of study selection.Fig 1:

sec.title: Description of the included studies
sec.p: Although the majority of studies did not report participants' mean bone mineral density T-scores, all fulfilled the inclusion criterion of enrolling individuals with a clinical diagnosis of osteopenia (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry T-score between −1.0 and −2.4) or osteoporosis (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry T-score of ≤ −2.5); only two trials explicitly provided the cohort's mean T-score. The age range of included participants spanned 40–87 years, with the largest proportion of participants concentrated between 60 and 75 years (approximately 50 % of the overall sample). Included studies were conducted in 16 countries across Asia (China, Japan, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, South Korea, Thailand), Europe (Austria, Germany, Italy, Sweden, Turkey, United Kingdom), North America (Canada, United States), South America (Brazil), and Oceania (Australia).

Based on exercise modality, we classified the 30 included randomized controlled trials into seven intervention types. When an intervention contained multiple components, we categorized it according to its primary stated goal in the original research: one aerobic exercise (Behrens et al., 2017), three balance-focused programs (Halvarsson et al., 2015; Miko, 2018; Stolzenberg et al., 2013), four resistance-training protocols (Hongo et al., 2007; Schröder and Banzer, 2012; Waltman et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022), five flexibility interventions (Pilates, Yoga, Tai Chi, Baduanjin) (Chyu et al., 2010; Grahn-Kronhed et al., 2020; Küçükçakır et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2015; Wayne et al., 2012), six impact-training regimens (Fischbacher et al., 2019; Greenway et al., 2015; Kistler-Fischbacher et al., 2021; Ng et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2015, 2018), and 11 functional-training schemes (Aveir et al., 2017; Bragonzoni et al., 2020; Burke et al., 2010; Ebid et al., 2021; Filipović et al., 2020, 2021; Hettchen et al., 2021; Hourigan et al., 2008; Kemmler et al., 2021; Liu-Ambrose et al., 2010; Posch et al., 2019). The duration of the exercise programs ranged from 10 to 64 weeks (median = 31 weeks, n = 30).

In the included studies, 50 % investigated the facilitators and barriers to exercise adherence. The most cited obstacle was 'lack of time' (n = 11/30) reported in most articles, indicating that it may be the primary barrier to exercise participation. Table 2, Table 3 summarize two key aspects. First, we present the frequency of influencing facilitators and barriers identified in the analyzed randomized controlled trials. Second, we outline the barriers, facilitators, and intervention goals related to exercise adherence.Table 2
Frequency table.Table 2
Facilitators (frequency)Inverse barriers (frequency)
Professional supervision & guidance (13)Lack of supervision or feedback (5)
Structured intervention design (11)Exercise intensity & complexity (4)
Social support & group interaction (9)Limited social support (1)
Perceived health benefits (6)Psychological concerns & lack of interest (4)
Safety assurance (5)Health-related limitations (10)
Convenience & accessibility (5)Time constraints (11)
Positive performance feedback (5)Lack of supervision or feedback (5)
Variety & enjoyment (2)Psychological concerns & lack of interest (4)
Family & work responsibilities (3)
Resource intensity & economic cost (2)Table 3
Barriers, facilitators and intervention targets for exercise adherence.Table 3:
Behavioral influence dimensionSpecific influencing factorsIntervention functionIntervention content
Capability
Physical capabilityBarrier: Cardiovascular instability, fractures, back pain, heart disease and other health issues; Minor and irrelevant damage; Withdrawal from exercise due to pain or discomfort; Low physical functionFacilitators: Professional supervision and personalized guidance for injuries; Risk mitigation strategyTraining; Enablement1. Provide professional guidance and movement demonstrations to address the issue of non-standard exercise execution;2. Develop a detailed exercise plan to enhance patient compliance and improve the condition of low physical function;3. Implement self-monitoring (recording exercise routines) to help avoid the risk of withdrawal caused by pain or discomfort;4. Conduct time management skills training and optimize the allocation of exercise time.
Psychological capabilityBarrier: Low self-efficacy; Lack of knowledge and skills related to sports; Fear of getting injured or fear of high-intensity exerciseFacilitators: Perceived significance of health benefits; Disease schema-driven motivationEducation; Training1. Explain the benefits of exercise for bone health, strengthen disease schema-driven motivation, and enhance the willingness to exercise;2. Teach exercise methods and precautions to fill the gap in knowledge and skills and reduce the fear of injury.
Motivation
Reflective motivationBarrier: Insufficient time; Unable to maintain high-intensity trainingFacilitators: Positive result feedback loop; The joy and inner appeal brought by diversityPersuasion; Incentivisation;Coercion1. By setting goals, enhance patients' compliance, clarify the training direction, and alleviate the difficulty of persisting in high-intensity training;2. Coaches or researchers provide feedback on sports performance to build a positive result feedback loop;3. Implement incentive and reward systems to enhance the intrinsic appeal of sports;4. Formulate strategic action plans, allocate time reasonably, and address the issue of insufficient time.
Intrinsic motivationBarrier: Decreased interest; Lack of motivation; Unable to persist; Afraid of inconvenienceFacilitators: Multi-mode training interface; The benefits of water medium for muscles and bonesIncentivisation; Coercion;Training1. Establish incentive and reward mechanisms to stimulate interest in sports and enhance intrinsic motivation;2. Teach exercise methods and precautions to reduce the inconvenience of exercise and help patients persist.
Opportunity
Physical opportunityBarrier: COVID-19 lockdown and fear of infection; Difficulty in moving around (including winter travel difficulties); Lack of suitable weather and traffic conditions; Equipment or facility restrictions; Family/work conflicts, care responsibilities and relocation; Physical disability;Facilitators: Environmental accessibility and convenienceEnvironmental restructuring; Restriction1. Enhance the accessibility of sports facilities, address the limitations of equipment/facilities, and facilitate continuous exercise;2. Make environmental adjustments (such as optimizing the indoor sports environment in winter and setting up home sports scenarios) to alleviate the travel difficulties caused by weather, traffic and lockdowns.
Social opportunityBarrier: Lack of family support; Lack of social support (including timely feedback and encouragement)Facilitators: Peer-mediated social support; Group interactionModelling; EnablementProvide social support and role demonstration in the form of team sports to make up for the lack of family support;2. Establish supportive social networks (such as patient sports communities) to provide timely feedback and encouragement.
Note: 1. Intervention functions target corresponding obstacle factors. 2. Intervention content is the practical form of intervention functions, working with promoting factors to boost patients' exercise participation and sustainability.

In 83 % of the included studies, methodologies for assessing exercise adherence and leveraging its facilitators were addressed. Of these, only four failed to report the adherence rate, and two did not specify the assessment methods employed. Adherence was typically calculated as the ratio of attended to scheduled sessions, supplemented by training diaries, telephone follow-ups, and supervised group sessions. Waltman et al. (2015) reported the lowest adherence at 58.9 %, whereas Posch et al. (2019) observed the highest, with 100 % adherence in the intervention group.

sec.title: Risk of bias in studies
sec.p:
The overall risk of bias among the included studies varied, as assessed by the Review Manager 5.4 tool, and all included studies involved a total of 30 independent sample groups (n = 30). (Fig. 2 presents detailed assessments of study quality.) Only nine studies were rated as having a low overall risk of bias, whereas the remaining studies were classified as having some concerns or a high risk of bias. Despite all included studies employing a randomized controlled trial design, most did not describe the allocation concealment procedure adequately. None of the studies reported blinding of participants sufficiently; however, 21 out of 30 trials (70 %) implemented blinding of outcome assessors. Twenty-two studies explicitly declared conflicts of interest, with 21 stating that no competing interests existed.Fig. 2
Summary of risk of bias across different types of biases.Fig 2:

sec.title: Summary of evidence
sec.p: Systematic synthesis identified three synergistic determinants influencing sustained exercise adherence among adults with osteoporosis: (1) Professional Guidance: Direct on-site supervision and continuous feedback from certified coaches or physiotherapists; (2) Program Design: A stepwise, participant-centered, time-sensitive, and interactive exercise protocol; (3) Environmental Support: Safe, accessible physical settings with ongoing peer reinforcement. These three elements were mutually reinforcing: professional oversight ensured scientific accuracy and safety, interactive programme design sustained engagement, and an accessible, safe environment, augmented by peer support, mitigated psychological barriers, such as fear of falling.

Although the above three factors synergistically promote exercise adherence, time constraints and health problems still actively impede initiation and maintenance (Arensman et al., 2024; Nevelikova et al., 2025). Future interventions must therefore reinforce these facilitators while directly targeting and resolving the barriers to maximise effectiveness. Notably, no prior studies have explicitly designed interventions based on this facilitator-barrier matrix, representing a critical research gap. Identifying these factors enables testing of practically applicable strategies to boost motivation and adherence. To clarify the mechanisms of behavioral change, we adopted the Behavior Change Wheel framework. We mapped deficits in capability/opportunity and motivational conflicts to specific intervention functions. This process helped outline tailored intervention content for the target population (Table 3).

When “unclear” judgments predominate, they usually reflect inadequate reporting rather than inherent methodological flaws; consequently, classifying studies as high or unclear risk based on insufficient detail may overestimate the true risk of bias.

Time constraints, personal health issues, and environmental obstacles−frequently cited barriers−are herein conceptualized as a unified thematic set. Consistent with our findings, researchers studying movement disorders have similarly identified time scarcity as a significant barrier across diverse populations, including those with osteopenia or osteoporosis (Baert et al., 2015; Pinheiro et al., 2020). However, the nature of time constraints−whether actual or perceived−remains unclear. Perceived time inadequacy is strongly influenced by reflective motivation, including competing daily responsibilities: individuals may perceive time scarcity despite objective availability when prioritizing more engaging tasks. Further research is needed to clarify whether these perceptions reflect objective reality or are influenced by unmeasured factors. If actual time constraints are confirmed, interventions should focus on flexible scheduling. For perceived time scarcity, targeted interventions can be developed using the functions outlined in Table 3. Our findings align with Ziebart et al.'s (2018) qualitative study, which similarly identified time scarcity as a primary barrier. Notably, Ziebart et al. (2018) highlighted "fear of injury" as the most significant barrier, whereas this factor was mentioned in only two of the included randomized controlled trials. This discrepancy may stem from limited data collection methods: included randomized controlled trials primarily reported physiological outcomes and adherence metrics, without validated psychological tools to measure fear of movement. Notably, Ziebart et al. (2018) explored a broader concept of physical activity. This is defined as any skeletal muscle movement that consumes energy (Baert et al., 2015). In contrast, our study focused solely on randomized controlled trials involving structured exercise interventions. Thus, the divergent primary barriers may reflect our narrow focus on exercise interventions, excluding facilitators and barriers related to general physical activity—a critical limitation. Future studies should identify the most impactful facilitators and barriers to exercise adherence in osteoporosis populations.

When new health issues arise during exercise, participants may need to cease their activity. Even though the exercise is intended to enhance their physical well-being, pain or injury during the session can interrupt program continuity and increase participant burden. Researchers who conducted a meta-anlysis on exercise interventions for frail older adults also indicated that co-existing illnesses, physical constraints, and negative psychological states diminished their inclination to engage in physical activity(Zhou et al., 2025). To address the impact of health issues on adherence, we propose the following solutions: If the pain from exercise is due to a disease or injury, adjust the exercise method according to the location and cause of the pain to prevent excessive strain and harm to the affected area. Establish both short-term and long-term movement goals that are specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound. Adhering to the principle of progressive improvement, begin with low-intensity exercises and slowly increase the intensity and duration.

Not all studies reported adherence rates, with only 76 % providing clear statements. Adherence numerators were inconsistently normalized (intention-to-treat versus completers), which prevented meta-analytic pooling. Posch et al. (2019) and Ebid et al. (2021) reported the highest adherence rates (100 %), potentially due to additional training sessions, a pleasant atmosphere, positive feedback, ensuring participant safety, supervised group courses three times a week with professional physical therapist guidance, and encouraging a daily 30-minute walk (family exercise plan). Greenway et al. (2015) had the lowest exercise adherence ('low hip' group: average adherence was 46 %, 'low spine' group: average adherence was 59 %). The specific reasons for the low exercise adherence were not clearly identified; however, possible influencing factors can be inferred from the exercise implementation and related background mentioned in the paper. The researchers adopted a family-based exercise program, which lacked the supervision and guidance of on-site professionals. Participants were required to exercise at home, but the authors do not address whether the family environment was conducive to the program. Barriers reported include five categories: family environment-related (family problems, moving away from the city), health-related (exercise-related pain, health problems, fear of falling), exercise-related (exercise intensity, exercise type, exercise schedule), external environment-related (bad weather, transportation facilities, sports venues, and facilities), and personal psychological-related (lack of interest, time constraints, lack of self-confidence, lack of motivation, and social factors).

Notably, the barriers limiting exercise adherence mirror those undermining pharmacological adherence, a second key determinant of osteoporosis management success (Cornelissen et al., 2020). Side-effects, financial burden, and regimen complexity drive these deficits (Doya et al., 2024; Jaleel et al., 2018), and they overlap substantially with the barriers to exercise adherence—namely, health complaints and resource constraints. Therefore, individualized care plans must proactively integrate and align exercise and pharmacological strategies to comprehensively improve patient outcomes.

Patient perceptions of exercise programs and preferences were insufficiently addressed: specifically, while one paper mentioned general perceptions (e.g., “The exercise I use in my daily routine is beneficial to me”), none comprehensively evaluated these variables. Evaluating and incorporating participants’ exercise preferences would enhance intervention appeal, and researchers conducting future randomized controlled trials should systematically assess these factors.

Socioeconomic status also emerged as an understudied determinant. Our review found socioeconomic status influenced both exercise time and personal health of osteoporosis patients; individuals with low socioeconomic status faced barriers like financial constraints, family responsibilities, and resource scarcity, while those with high socioeconomic status reported barriers such as work pressure, social expectations, and unhealthy lifestyles (Assembly et al., 2010; Pedersen et al., 2021). Future researchers should further explore socioeconomic status-specific barriers to tailor interventions accordingly.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: To improve retrieval efficiency, reduce costs, ensure the quality of the literature, and enhance the rigor, reproducibility, and verifiability of the research, only English-language studies were included. This approach may have introducted language bias, potentially overlooking important research findings in non-English-language literature and affecting the application of our research findings in non-English-speaking countries. Furthermore, most researchers focused on female populations, so our findings may not be generalizable to male populations. Lastly, the terms “exercise” and “physical activity” are often used interchangeably in the literature, which may cause some confusion when comparing studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In this review, patients with osteoporosis faced numerous challenges in engaging in physical exercise, among which time constraints and personal health issues were the most common barriers to exercise. However, we have identified several factors that promote exercise participation, including professional guidance and supervision, interactive and enjoyable exercise programs, group training and social interaction, accessible exercise facilities, and comprehensive safety measures. These factors provided support to patients from different dimensions, thereby enhancing their adherence to and enthusiasm for exercise programs.

From the perspective of the Behavior Change Wheel theory, the key facilitators identified correspond to the three key elements of capability, opportunity, and motivation. Professional guidance and supervision enhance patients' capability by enabling them to master correct exercise methods and techniques, thereby boosting their confidence and effectiveness in exercise. The interactivity and enjoyment of exercise programs, as well as group training and social interaction, can stimulate patients' motivation, allowing them to experience pleasure and social support during exercise and thus be more willing to continue participating. Accessible exercise facilities and safety measures provide patients with favorable opportunities, reducing external barriers to exercise and making it easier for them to engage in physical activity.

However, in most studies, the methods for leveraging facilitators, addressing barriers, and measuring exercise adherence were underemphasized, and there was significant variation in the types and intensity of exercise. This indicates that current researchers have not yet explored effective ways to promote exercise adherence among patients with osteoporosis in a sufficiently deep and systematic manner, lacking unified standards and methods.

Researchers should base studies on the Behavior Change Wheel theory and comprehensively consider the three dimensions of capability, opportunity, and motivation to thoroughly investigate the most effective exercise modalities for patients with osteoporosis. At the same time, it is necessary to further clarify the factors that promote and hinder exercise, especially to determine whether time constraints and personal health issues are the main barriers to exercise in this population, as well as the specific manifestations and impact of these barriers in different patient groups. On this basis, prospective randomized controlled trials can be designed to incorporate mixed-methods approaches (qualitative + quantitative) to better capture the reasons for patient adherence or dropout, and optimize and capitalize on facilitators, while specifically addressing barriers, thereby improving patients’ exercise adherence. For example, personalized exercise guidance and training can be provided to address capability deficits; the accessibility and safety of exercise facilities can be improved to address opportunity shortages; and more enjoyable and socially interactive exercise programs can be designed to address motivational deficiencies. Through this systematic research and intervention based on the Behavior Change Wheel theory, more scientific and effective exercise support programs can be provided for patients with osteoporosis, thereby improving their exercise participation and overall health status.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Yi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chenxin Zhang: Data curation. Liping Zhang: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xian Ma: Conceptualization. Zhenghui Dong: Supervision.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
